stem:
A 5-month-old, term, previously well boy is admitted to your general paediatric ward with 3 days of coryza, cough, and progressive work of breathing. His mother says 'he just won't take the bottle anymore and his ribs are sucking in when he breathes'. He is breastfed with some formula top-ups and is wet nappies five times per day.

On arrival 90 minutes ago he was started on nasal prong oxygen 1 L/min for SpO2 88% on room air; his saturation rose to 93% within 10 minutes. He is afebrile, alert, and well-perfused. Auscultation shows widespread fine crepitations and a few expiratory wheezes with good air entry. His subcostal recession has not improved despite the low-flow oxygen and his oral intake is now under 50% of normal volume. Capillary blood gas shows pH 7.34 (7.35-7.45), pCO2 50 mmHg (35-45), and bicarbonate 24 mmol/L (22-26). His nasopharyngeal swab is positive for respiratory syncytial virus.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 168, BP 88/52 mmHg, RR 64, SpO2 93% on 1 L/min nasal prongs, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, well-perfused, marked subcostal recession, fine widespread crepitations, scattered expiratory wheeze, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Investigations: Capillary pH 7.34, pCO2 50 mmHg (35-45), bicarbonate 24 mmol/L (22-26), NPA positive for RSV
data_table.Medications: Nasal prong oxygen 1 L/min for the past 90 minutes; no bronchodilators given

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue nasal prong oxygen at 1 L/min and reassess work of breathing again in another 2 hours
options.rationale: Low-flow oxygen has reached its ceiling and has not improved work of breathing or feeding; the AU bronchiolitis pathway escalates after failure rather than waiting further. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula therapy at 2 L/kg/min titrated to a target oxygen saturation of 90% or above
options.rationale: RCH bronchiolitis guidance escalates to high-flow nasal cannula after failure of low-flow oxygen, defined as ongoing increased work of breathing, feeding compromise, or rising pCO2 despite 1-2 L/min for at least 2 hours. The saturation target is 90% or above (not 92-94%) and the flow is set at 2 L/kg/min. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; PARIS trial)

options.text: Trial nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg back-to-back with nebulised 3% hypertonic saline as a respiratory adjunct
options.rationale: Salbutamol and hypertonic saline are not recommended in routine bronchiolitis under 12 months; trials have not shown a length-of-stay benefit. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer a single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg with airway clearance physiotherapy added concurrently
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not improve bronchiolitis outcomes in this age group; chest physiotherapy is similarly not routine. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Intubate and ventilate now for the rising carbon dioxide of 50 mmHg and the persistent increased work of breathing
options.rationale: A pCO2 of 50 mmHg with a near-normal pH does not by itself mandate intubation; high-flow nasal cannula is the next escalation before invasive ventilation in stable bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is a viral lower respiratory tract infection (RSV predominant) peaking at age 2-6 months. Australian (RCH) guidance starts oxygen at low-flow nasal prongs 1-2 L/min when SpO2 is under 90% and escalates to high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min when low-flow fails after at least 2 hours, with a target SpO2 of 90% or above. Salbutamol, corticosteroids, hypertonic saline, antibiotics, and physiotherapy are not routine. Failure of high-flow nasal cannula prompts paediatric intensive care escalation.
key_points: Low-flow oxygen first at 1-2 L/min; target SpO2 >=90%
Escalate to HFNC at 2 L/kg/min after at least 2 hours of low-flow failure
Salbutamol, steroids, hypertonic saline NOT routine
Failure of HFNC: PICU escalation
explanation.pearls: In bronchiolitis under 12 months the AU SpO2 target is 90% (not 92-94%) - lower targets reduce overtreatment without harm in well-perfused infants.

stem:
A 2-year-old boy is brought to your emergency department at 0200 with a barking cough and noisy breathing. His mother says 'he sounded like a seal barking and now he's making this loud noise when he breathes in, even when he's just lying still'. He has had 2 days of coryza. He has had no contact with anyone unwell at home.

On examination he is alert but anxious, sitting on his mother's lap. He has audible stridor at rest with marked suprasternal and intercostal recession. He is moving air symmetrically with no wheeze. Oxygen saturation is 96% on room air. Capillary refill is under 2 seconds. He has not been febrile and he is well-hydrated. He has had no medication so far. The croup score (Westley) is calculated at 7, with stridor at rest, marked recession, and decreased air entry without cyanosis.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 140, BP 96/60 mmHg, RR 36, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 37.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert anxious, sitting on mother's lap, stridor at rest, marked suprasternal and intercostal recession, no wheeze, symmetrical air entry
data_table.Investigations: Westley croup score 7 (severe); no blood tests indicated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg only and review the work of breathing in 30 minutes time
options.rationale: Mild and moderate croup are treated with dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg, but severe croup with stridor at rest requires a higher dose (0.6 mg/kg) and concurrent nebulised adrenaline. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Give nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 plus oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg and observe for at least 3 hours
options.rationale: Severe croup (Westley score 6 or above, stridor at rest with marked recession) receives concurrent nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 (a flat dose, not weight-based) and dexamethasone at the higher 0.6 mg/kg oral dose. Children are observed for at least 3 hours after the last adrenaline dose to detect rebound. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Intubate now for impending respiratory failure with persistent stridor and intercostal recession at rest
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for severe croup that fails nebulised adrenaline and steroids or shows exhaustion, altered consciousness, or cyanosis; first-line pharmacological treatment has not yet been trialled. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Administer nebulised budesonide 2 mg as the sole steroid agent and arrange admission for observation
options.rationale: Nebulised budesonide is an alternative when oral dexamethasone cannot be given, but is not first-line and does not replace adrenaline in severe disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Discharge home with a written information sheet on humidified air and a paracetamol dosing schedule
options.rationale: Severe croup with stridor at rest requires pharmacological treatment and at least 3 hours of monitored observation; discharge before adrenaline trial is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

explanation.summary: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis) is a viral upper-airway infection typically in children aged 6 months to 6 years. The Westley score (5 components - level of consciousness, cyanosis, stridor, air entry, retraction) grades mild (under 3), moderate (3-5), severe (6-11), and impending failure (12 or above). Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg orally is given for mild or moderate croup; 0.6 mg/kg is reserved for severe disease. Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 (flat dose) is added in severe disease and any child is observed for at least 3 hours after the last adrenaline dose.
key_points: Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg for mild/moderate; 0.6 mg/kg for severe
Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 (flat dose)
Observation at least 3 hours after last adrenaline
Westley score >=6 = severe
explanation.pearls: Nebulised adrenaline is a temporising measure; without concurrent dexamethasone the stridor can rebound within 2-3 hours.

stem:
A 4-month-old boy is admitted to your paediatric ward with fever to 39.2 °C and irritability. His mother says 'he just goes red and screams when he wees and his nappies have been really smelly for two days'. He has had no cough, no diarrhoea, and no rash. He was born at term and was not screened on antenatal ultrasound for renal anomalies because his mother had no antenatal care.

On examination he is alert but unsettled, with a soft abdomen and no flank mass. Urinalysis from a clean catch sample shows leukocytes 3+ and nitrites positive; microscopy reports more than 100 white cells per high-power field. Empirical intravenous gentamicin plus benzylpenicillin was started 48 hours ago. He remains febrile to 38.6 °C and is still feeding poorly. Urine culture has now returned showing Klebsiella pneumoniae at 10^8 CFU/mL with the gentamicin being the only sensitive listed antibiotic; the sensitivity strip confirms susceptibility.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 168, BP 86/52 mmHg, RR 42, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 38.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, unsettled, soft abdomen, no flank mass, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis leukocytes 3+ nitrites positive, microscopy WCC >100/HPF, urine culture Klebsiella pneumoniae 10^8 CFU/mL gentamicin-sensitive, CRP 96 mg/L (under 5), creatinine 36 micromol/L (15-37)
data_table.Medications: Intravenous gentamicin once daily plus benzylpenicillin for 48 hours

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue current intravenous antibiotics and arrange a renal ultrasound scan during the current admission
options.rationale: This child has atypical UTI features (under 6 months of age, non-E coli pathogen, poor response within 48 hours of appropriate antibiotics, no antenatal renal ultrasound documentation). RCH guidance triggers a renal tract ultrasound during the current admission for atypical UTI. DMSA and MCUG are not part of the RCH cascade. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Continue intravenous antibiotics and arrange a dimercaptosuccinic acid scan in 6 months as routine assessment
options.rationale: DMSA is a NICE-derived investigation and is not part of the RCH AU UTI imaging cascade. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Switch to oral co-trimoxazole prophylaxis only and arrange outpatient renal ultrasound in 6 weeks time
options.rationale: Oral switch before clinical response and before imaging risks missing structural abnormality; prophylaxis alone is not first-line. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Continue intravenous antibiotics and arrange a micturating cystourethrogram during the current admission
options.rationale: MCUG is not part of the RCH cascade and is reserved for selected paediatric urology cases; AU first-line imaging is the renal tract ultrasound. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Cease intravenous antibiotics, observe off treatment, and repeat urine culture in 48 hours from a fresh sample
options.rationale: Ceasing antibiotics in a confirmed UTI with poor response risks worsening pyelonephritis and bacteraemia; escalation not cessation is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: Australian (RCH) paediatric UTI guidance reserves renal tract ultrasound for atypical UTI (poor antibiotic response within 48 hours, non-E coli, sepsis, abdominal mass, raised creatinine) or recurrent UTI. Infants under 6 months also receive imaging where no antenatal ultrasound coverage has been documented. DMSA and MCUG are NICE constructs and are not part of the RCH cascade. Single typical UTI in a child over 6 months with rapid response does not need routine imaging. The intravenous antibiotic course continues until clinical response and culture sensitivities guide the oral switch.
key_points: Ultrasound during admission for atypical UTI or under 6 months without antenatal imaging
Atypical features include non-E coli, poor 48-hour response, sepsis
DMSA and MCUG NOT in the RCH cascade
Single typical UTI over 6 months does not need routine imaging
explanation.pearls: Recurrent UTI (two or more upper UTIs, or one upper plus one or more lower) triggers ultrasound regardless of age; DMSA still has no place in the RCH algorithm.

stem:
A 9-year-old girl with known persistent asthma is in your paediatric resuscitation bay with severe acute asthma. Her mother says 'her chest is sucking in and she can hardly say a word'. She has had three back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with ipratropium bromide 250 mcg added to each dose, and was given oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg 40 minutes ago. She has had a normal chest X-ray and there is no pneumothorax.

On examination she remains using accessory muscles with persistent subcostal recession and only marginally improved tachypnoea. Her air entry is fair throughout with persistent expiratory wheeze; the chest is not silent. She is GCS 15 but tired. Cardiac monitoring shows sinus tachycardia at 156. Her capillary blood gas shows pH 7.30 (7.35-7.45), pCO2 46 mmHg (35-45), bicarbonate 22 mmol/L (22-26). She has no history of intubation. Intravenous access is established and her potassium is 3.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.0).

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 156, BP 102/64 mmHg, RR 38, SpO2 91% on 6 L/min mask oxygen, temperature 37.0 °C
data_table.Examination: Sitting upright, tired, single words, accessory muscle use, persistent expiratory wheeze with fair air entry, no silent chest, GCS 15
data_table.Investigations: Capillary pH 7.30, pCO2 46 mmHg (35-45), bicarbonate 22 mmol/L (22-26), potassium 3.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), chest X-ray clear
data_table.Medications: Three back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg plus ipratropium 250 mcg; oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg 40 minutes ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg as a slow infusion over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Severe paediatric asthma that fails the initial back-to-back salbutamol plus ipratropium plus oral steroid step is escalated with intravenous magnesium sulfate at 50 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) infused over 20 minutes under cardiac monitoring. Aminophylline is the next step thereafter if magnesium fails. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma; National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook)
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Commence intravenous aminophylline loading dose 10 mg/kg over 1 hour and then a maintenance infusion
options.rationale: Aminophylline is the next step after magnesium failure, not before; the narrow therapeutic window and adverse effect profile place it lower on the escalation ladder. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

options.text: Add nebulised hypertonic saline 3% as a respiratory adjunct and continue continuous nebulised salbutamol
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline is not indicated in acute asthma; the AU escalation step is intravenous magnesium sulfate. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

options.text: Intubate and commence mechanical ventilation with permissive hypercapnia and inhaled volatile anaesthesia
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for life-threatening features (silent chest, exhaustion, altered consciousness, cardiopulmonary arrest); the child here is still moving air with fair entry and is GCS 15. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

options.text: Commence non-invasive ventilation with bi-level positive airway pressure as the next escalation modality
options.rationale: Non-invasive ventilation is not standard of care in paediatric severe asthma in Australia; the next pharmacological step is intravenous magnesium sulfate. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

explanation.summary: Severe paediatric acute asthma is treated with continuous oxygen, back-to-back salbutamol plus ipratropium for the first hour, and oral or intravenous corticosteroids. Failure of this initial therapy escalates to intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring, then aminophylline if magnesium fails. Life-threatening features (silent chest, exhaustion, altered consciousness) mandate paediatric intensive care escalation and consideration of intubation. The capillary blood gas trend is a better marker than a single value.
key_points: Salbutamol plus ipratropium plus steroid for first hour
Magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg IV (max 2 g) next
Aminophylline reserved for magnesium failure
Life-threatening features: silent chest, exhaustion, altered consciousness
explanation.pearls: Hypokalaemia from salbutamol use is common in severe asthma and is monitored on serial venous gases; potassium replacement is given when below 3.5 mmol/L during continuous bronchodilator therapy.

sources.label: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

stem:
A 4-year-old boy is brought to your emergency department after eating a peanut-containing biscuit at a birthday party 20 minutes ago. His mother says 'his face puffed up, he came out in hives and now he's wheezing and saying he can't breathe properly'. He has had two prior reactions to peanuts treated with antihistamines without adrenaline. He has been previously well and is not on any regular medications.

On examination he has facial and periorbital angioedema, generalised urticaria, and audible wheeze with intercostal recession. He is alert but anxious. His weight is documented as 16 kg from a recent immunisation visit. Intravenous access has not yet been established. He has had no prior fluid bolus. Oxygen is being delivered by mask at 6 L/min. The ED nursing team is preparing intramuscular adrenaline at your direction. There is no inspiratory stridor at rest yet.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 148, BP 80/46 mmHg, RR 36, SpO2 92% on 6 L/min mask oxygen, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert anxious, facial and periorbital angioedema, generalised urticaria, audible wheeze with intercostal recession, no stridor at rest
data_table.Investigations: Documented weight 16 kg, no IV access yet

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first dose of adrenaline?

options.text: Adrenaline 150 microgram (EpiPen Junior equivalent) intramuscularly into the anterolateral thigh now
options.rationale: ASCIA paediatric anaphylaxis dosing uses banded intramuscular adrenaline by weight, not weight-based arithmetic. EpiPen Junior 150 microgram covers children 10-20 kg; EpiPen 300 microgram covers children 20-50 kg. A 16 kg child falls into the EpiPen Junior band. The dose may be repeated every 5 minutes if needed. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Acute Management Guidelines)

options.text: Adrenaline 160 microgram (10 microgram/kg) intramuscularly into the anterolateral thigh now as a calculated dose
options.rationale: Weight-based arithmetic is not the AU standard; the ASCIA banded table protects against dose errors during anaphylaxis. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Acute Management Guidelines)

options.text: Adrenaline 300 microgram (EpiPen equivalent) intramuscularly into the anterolateral thigh now
options.rationale: EpiPen 300 microgram is the band for children 20-50 kg; this child at 16 kg is in the EpiPen Junior band (150 microgram). (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Acute Management Guidelines)

options.text: Adrenaline 16 microgram (1 microgram/kg) intravenously as a single bolus dose without an infusion
options.rationale: Intravenous adrenaline is reserved for refractory anaphylaxis with intravenous access and infusion pump capability; intramuscular adrenaline is the AU first-line. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Acute Management Guidelines)

options.text: Adrenaline 1.6 milligram (100 microgram/kg) nebulised via a paediatric nebuliser mask immediately
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is used for upper airway oedema with stridor as an adjunct; it is not a substitute for intramuscular adrenaline in systemic anaphylaxis. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Acute Management Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Anaphylaxis is treated with prompt intramuscular adrenaline to the anterolateral thigh. ASCIA's banded paediatric dosing avoids weight-based arithmetic during emergencies. EpiPen Junior 150 microgram covers 10-20 kg children, EpiPen 300 microgram covers 20-50 kg children, and 0.5 mg ampoule doses are used in adolescents and adults at 50 kg or more. Doses may be repeated every 5 minutes. Intramuscular is preferred over intravenous unless refractory; intravenous adrenaline requires an infusion pump and dilution to avoid arrhythmia.
key_points: EpiPen Junior 150 microgram = 10-20 kg
EpiPen 300 microgram = 20-50 kg
Adolescent/adult 0.5 mg if 50 kg or more
Repeat every 5 minutes; IV requires infusion pump
explanation.pearls: Biphasic anaphylaxis occurs in roughly 1 in 20 cases at 4-12 hours after the initial reaction; ASCIA recommends observation for at least 4 hours after symptom resolution before discharge.

stem:
A 2-year-old girl is brought to your paediatric ED with 36 hours of vomiting and watery diarrhoea. Her father says 'she keeps throwing up everything she sips, and her wee is dark like apple juice'. There is no blood in the stool. Her older sibling has had similar symptoms. She has had one wet nappy in the last 8 hours.

On examination she is alert but tired, with dry mucous membranes, a sunken anterior fontanelle (still partially open), and reduced skin turgor. Capillary refill is 2.5 seconds. She has no abdominal tenderness or peritonism. Her weight today is 11 kg, down from a documented baseline of 12 kg 3 weeks ago. A trial of small frequent oral rehydration solution (10 mL every 5 minutes by syringe) was given for 60 minutes; she has vomited each time and continues to refuse fluids. Bedside finger-prick glucose is 4.6 mmol/L. A single oral ondansetron wafer 2 mg was given 30 minutes ago without effect on the vomiting.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 144, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 26, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 37.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert tired, dry mucous membranes, partially sunken fontanelle, reduced skin turgor, capillary refill 2.5 seconds, soft non-tender abdomen
data_table.Investigations: Bedside finger-prick glucose 4.6 mmol/L; weight 11 kg down from 12 kg (estimated 8% dehydration)
data_table.Medications: Single oral ondansetron 2 mg given 30 minutes ago without effect

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Insert a nasogastric tube and start continuous rehydration with oral rehydration solution at 25 mL/kg/hour for 4 hours
options.rationale: RCH guidance for moderate dehydration that has failed oral rehydration uses nasogastric tube rehydration as first-line ahead of intravenous fluids, with oral rehydration solution at 25 mL/kg/hour over 4 hours. NGT is preferred over IV because of reduced complications, lower cost, and equivalent outcomes. IV is reserved for severe dehydration, shock, ileus, or NGT failure. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Commence intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride bolus 20 mL/kg followed by a maintenance infusion immediately
options.rationale: Intravenous fluid is reserved for severe dehydration, shock, persistent vomiting after NGT trial, ileus, or electrolyte derangement requiring controlled correction; NGT rehydration is the AU first-line for moderate dehydration that fails oral. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Continue trial of oral rehydration solution at 5 mL every 5 minutes for a further 2 hours of observation
options.rationale: She has already failed an adequate trial of oral rehydration and is continuing to vomit; persisting risks further deterioration without correcting the deficit. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Administer a repeat dose of oral ondansetron wafer 4 mg and continue the oral fluid trial only
options.rationale: Repeat ondansetron does not address the existing fluid deficit; a single dose is the recommended ceiling per encounter and dose escalation is not the AU pathway. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Send stool sample for culture and viral testing and discharge home with oral rehydration sachets for parents
options.rationale: Discharge with established 8% dehydration is unsafe; rehydration must be completed in hospital before any consideration of discharge. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

explanation.summary: Paediatric gastroenteritis dehydration is graded mild (under 4%), moderate (4-6%), or severe (over 7%) by clinical signs. The Australian (RCH) pathway uses oral rehydration first; failed oral rehydration moves to nasogastric tube rehydration at 25 mL/kg/hour for 4 hours, not intravenous fluids. Intravenous rehydration is reserved for severe dehydration with shock, persistent ileus, NGT failure, or electrolyte derangement requiring controlled correction. Ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg as a single dose (weight-banded: 2 mg if under 15 kg, 4 mg if 15-30 kg) reduces vomiting and admission rates.
key_points: Mild under 4%; moderate 4-6%; severe over 7%
NGT rehydration at 25 mL/kg/hour for 4 hours is first after oral failure
IV reserved for severe dehydration, shock, ileus, NGT failure
Single dose ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg (banded by weight)
explanation.pearls: NGT rehydration in moderate paediatric dehydration has equivalent outcomes to intravenous rehydration with fewer complications and reduced length of stay; RCH explicitly prefers it.

stem:
A 16-month-old Aboriginal boy is brought to your remote primary care clinic with 24 hours of fever to 39.0 °C and ear-pulling. His mother says 'he keeps grabbing at his right ear and crying when he lies down to sleep'. He has had no diarrhoea, no rash, and no respiratory distress. He is fully immunised. He attends a remote community kindergarten and has had three previous episodes of acute otitis media in the past 6 months.

On examination both tympanic membranes are bulging and erythematous, with loss of light reflex on the right. There is no perforation and no purulent discharge. The neck is supple, the throat is unremarkable, and the chest is clear. His hydration is satisfactory. He is otherwise alert and consoles in his mother's lap. The clinic has access to oral amoxicillin and intramuscular ceftriaxone but not a same-day audiology service. The community has high rates of chronic suppurative otitis media in older children.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 138, BP 96/60 mmHg, RR 26, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 39.0 °C
data_table.Examination: Both tympanic membranes bulging and erythematous, loss of light reflex right, no perforation, no discharge, supple neck, clear chest

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Prescribe high-dose oral amoxicillin 50 mg/kg/day in two divided doses for 7 days and review in person at 48 hours
options.rationale: In Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children, otitis media is treated with antibiotics immediately rather than watchful waiting because of the high background risk of perforation and chronic suppurative otitis media. The AU first-line is oral amoxicillin 50 mg/kg/day divided BD for 7 days. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; CARPA Standard Treatment Manual)

options.text: Adopt watchful waiting with regular paracetamol and review in 48 hours if symptoms persist or worsen
options.rationale: Watchful waiting is reasonable in low-risk non-Indigenous children over 2 years with mild unilateral disease, not in Aboriginal children in remote settings where perforation rates are high. (Source: CARPA Standard Treatment Manual)

options.text: Administer a single intramuscular ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg dose now as initial therapy and review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is reserved for severe disease, vomiting, or failure of first-line amoxicillin in this age group; first-line is oral amoxicillin. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Prescribe oral co-amoxiclav 90 mg/kg/day amoxicillin component divided three times daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Co-amoxiclav is reserved for treatment failure at 48-72 hours or recurrent otitis media despite adequate first-line therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Refer urgently for tympanocentesis and culture of middle ear fluid before commencing any antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Tympanocentesis is reserved for treatment failure, immunocompromise, or complicated disease; empirical antibiotics are first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Acute otitis media in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children carries a much higher risk of progression to perforation and chronic suppurative otitis media than in non-Indigenous children, and watchful waiting is not appropriate as initial therapy. Australian (CARPA/Therapeutic Guidelines) first-line is high-dose oral amoxicillin 50 mg/kg/day divided twice daily for 7 days, with in-person review at 48 hours. Treatment failure escalates to co-amoxiclav and audiology follow-up. Recurrent otitis media (three episodes in 6 months or four in 12) prompts ENT referral and consideration of grommets.
key_points: No watchful waiting for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children
First-line: high-dose amoxicillin 50 mg/kg/day BD for 7 days
Failure at 48-72 hours: switch to co-amoxiclav
Recurrent disease: ENT referral and grommet consideration
explanation.pearls: Audiology assessment after recurrent acute otitis media is essential in remote Australia because of the long-term language and learning impact of even mild conductive hearing loss in early childhood.

stem:
You are the paediatric registrar on call. A 22-month-old girl is on the general paediatric ward 36 hours after a first dose of intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg for complete Kawasaki disease (8 days of fever, conjunctivitis, oral changes, peripheral oedema, polymorphous rash). The bedside nurse calls you because she has spiked a fever to 39.2 °C again 12 hours after the IVIG completed. Her CRP, which had fallen to 84 mg/L at the end of the infusion, is now 138 mg/L on a repeat measurement.

On examination she remains miserable, with persistent dry fissured lips and bilateral conjunctival injection. There is no new petechiae, no shock, and no new murmur. Her aspirin 80 mg/kg/day in four divided doses is ongoing. The baseline echocardiogram at 24 hours showed no coronary artery dilatation (Z-score plus 0.6 in the left main coronary, plus 0.4 in the right). Her family is from a Japanese background and her parents ask 'is there something stronger now?'. She has had no live vaccines in the last 4 weeks.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 154, BP 102/64 mmHg, RR 32, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 39.2 °C
data_table.Examination: Miserable, persistent dry fissured lips, bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, no shock, no new murmur, no new rash
data_table.Investigations: CRP 138 mg/L rising from 84 mg/L post-IVIG, albumin 27 g/L (35-50), echocardiogram coronary Z-score plus 0.6 LMCA plus 0.4 RCA at 24 hours
data_table.Medications: IVIG 2 g/kg completed 12 hours ago; aspirin 80 mg/kg/day divided four times daily ongoing

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg as the next step in escalation for IVIG-resistant Kawasaki disease
options.rationale: IVIG-resistant Kawasaki disease (persistent or recurrent fever at 36 hours or more after the first IVIG infusion) is treated with a second IVIG dose 2 g/kg as the AU first-line escalation. Intravenous methylprednisolone pulses, oral prednisolone, and infliximab are reserved for second IVIG failure or high coronary aneurysm risk scores. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Scientific Statement; RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Administer intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg pulse daily for 3 days as the second-line escalation step
options.rationale: Pulsed methylprednisolone is reserved for second IVIG failure or high-risk scores at baseline; the AU first escalation is a second IVIG. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Add intravenous infliximab 5 mg/kg single dose as the second-line escalation step ahead of further IVIG
options.rationale: Infliximab is third-line in Australia after second IVIG and pulsed steroids have failed; placing it first is not the recommended order. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Continue current therapy and observe for a further 48 hours before any decision to escalate treatment
options.rationale: Persistent fever beyond 36 hours after IVIG defines IVIG resistance and requires escalation; observation increases coronary aneurysm risk. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Scientific Statement)

options.text: Cease aspirin and commence anticoagulation with low-molecular-weight heparin instead pending repeat echocardiogram
options.rationale: Aspirin is continued through IVIG resistance unless coronary aneurysms over 8 mm in diameter develop, when adjunctive anticoagulation is added; aspirin is not ceased. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Scientific Statement)

explanation.summary: IVIG-resistant Kawasaki disease is defined by persistent or recurrent fever 36 hours or more after the first IVIG dose has completed. About 10-20% of children meet this definition. The AU and AHA first-line escalation is a second IVIG dose 2 g/kg. If that fails, pulsed intravenous methylprednisolone or infliximab is the next step. Aspirin is continued at high anti-inflammatory dose until inflammatory markers normalise (typically 48-72 hours after fever resolution) before reducing to low-dose antiplatelet for at least 6-8 weeks. Echocardiography is repeated at 2 weeks and 6 weeks regardless of treatment course.
key_points: IVIG-resistant: fever 36+ hours after first IVIG dose
First escalation: second IVIG 2 g/kg
Second escalation: pulsed methylprednisolone or infliximab
Echocardiography at 2 and 6 weeks regardless
explanation.pearls: Risk scores predicting IVIG resistance (Kobayashi, Egami) developed in Japanese cohorts do not perform well in non-Japanese populations; AU practice is to escalate empirically on the 36-hour fever rule rather than on a score.

stem:
You are the paediatric registrar called urgently to the ward. A 10-year-old girl was admitted 6 hours ago with new-onset type 1 diabetes mellitus presenting in diabetic ketoacidosis (pH 7.05, bicarbonate 6 mmol/L, glucose 32 mmol/L, ketones 5.8 mmol/L). She has received 10 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride as a single bolus, then 0.9% sodium chloride at a 48-hour rehydration rate, plus a fixed-rate insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/hour. Her glucose is now 14 mmol/L and her pH has improved to 7.18.

The nurse says 'she's been a bit harder to rouse over the last 20 minutes and her headache is worse'. On examination her GCS has dropped from 15 to 13 (eyes open to voice, confused conversation). Her pulse is 64 (down from 122 on arrival), blood pressure is 142/88 mmHg (up from 102/60), and respiratory rate has slowed from 38 to 22. Pupils are 4 mm and equal, with sluggish reactions. The fontanelle is closed. There is no focal neurological deficit. Bedside glucose is now 12 mmol/L; ketones 4.1 mmol/L; bicarbonate 12 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 64 (was 122), BP 142/88 mmHg (was 102/60), RR 22 (was 38), SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: GCS 13 (eyes open to voice, confused), pupils 4 mm equal sluggish, no focal deficit, fontanelle closed
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 12 mmol/L, ketones 4.1 mmol/L, bicarbonate 12 mmol/L (was 6)
data_table.Medications: Fixed-rate insulin infusion 0.05 units/kg/hour and 0.9% sodium chloride at 48-hour rehydration rate

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intravenous mannitol 1 g/kg over 20 minutes and reduce intravenous fluid rate by one-third immediately
options.rationale: Cerebral oedema is the leading cause of paediatric DKA mortality; the diagnostic triad of declining GCS, bradycardia with hypertension, and slowed respiration is the Cushing reflex. Hypertonic saline 3% (2.5-5 mL/kg) or mannitol (0.5-1 g/kg over 20 minutes) is given immediately; intravenous fluids are reduced by one-third; insulin infusion is continued but the rate may be lowered. Neuro-imaging follows stabilisation, not precedes it. (Source: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines on DKA; RCH CPG: Diabetic ketoacidosis)
source_refs: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines on DKA

options.text: Increase the intravenous fluid rate to compensate for ongoing osmotic diuresis and continue insulin at the present rate
options.rationale: Increasing fluids in suspected cerebral oedema can worsen the oedema; the AU pathway reduces the rate by one-third while giving osmotherapy. (Source: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines on DKA)
source_refs: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines on DKA

options.text: Cease the insulin infusion entirely to allow biochemical stabilisation before any further osmotic therapy
options.rationale: Insulin is continued (possibly at reduced rate) to maintain ketone clearance; ceasing insulin causes acidosis rebound and does not treat cerebral oedema. (Source: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines on DKA)
source_refs: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines on DKA

options.text: Order an urgent computed tomography head scan before any intervention to confirm cerebral oedema or other cause
options.rationale: Imaging follows immediate osmotic therapy; the clinical syndrome with the Cushing reflex pattern is treated empirically without waiting for imaging. (Source: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines on DKA)
source_refs: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines on DKA

options.text: Administer intravenous dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg as a single dose for presumed inflammatory cerebral oedema
options.rationale: Steroids have no role in DKA cerebral oedema; the mechanism is osmotic and inflammatory steroid response is not part of the pathway. (Source: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines on DKA)
source_refs: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines on DKA

explanation.summary: Cerebral oedema is the leading cause of paediatric DKA mortality and morbidity. Risk factors include young age (under 5), severe acidosis at presentation (pH under 7.1), hyponatraemia, high urea, bicarbonate use, and rapid sodium or fluid administration. The diagnostic clinical triad is declining GCS, bradycardia with hypertension, and slowed respiration (Cushing reflex). Treatment is osmotherapy with hypertonic saline 3% (2.5-5 mL/kg) or mannitol (0.5-1 g/kg over 20 minutes), reduce intravenous fluid rate by one-third, head-up positioning, neuroprotection, and PICU transfer. Neuro-imaging is performed after stabilisation.
key_points: Cushing reflex: declining GCS plus bradycardia plus hypertension plus slowed respiration
Hypertonic saline 3% (2.5-5 mL/kg) OR mannitol (0.5-1 g/kg) over 20 min
Reduce IV fluid rate by one-third while giving osmotherapy
Imaging after stabilisation, never before treatment
explanation.pearls: Effective serum osmolality (2 x sodium plus glucose) under 320 mosm/kg in a child with DKA who deteriorates is highly suggestive of cerebral oedema rather than dehydration.

sources.label: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines on DKA

stem:
You are the paediatric registrar called to the postnatal ward. A 36-hour-old, term, exclusively breastfed boy has had two episodes of green vomiting in the last 4 hours. The midwife says 'his vomits have gone from milk-coloured to bright green and his tummy looks a bit bloated now'. He had been feeding well until this morning and had one meconium pass at 18 hours of life.

On examination he is alert but quiet, with a mildly distended abdomen and no palpable mass. The abdomen is soft without peritonism. Bowel sounds are present. Anus is patent on inspection. The remaining examination is unremarkable. Bedside finger-prick glucose is 4.4 mmol/L. The mother had normal antenatal scans including a 20-week morphology scan; there was no polyhydramnios. He has had no syndromic features identified. A nasogastric tube placed on your direction drains 25 mL of bilious fluid. He has had no further meconium since the first pass.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 152, BP 72/44 mmHg, RR 48, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert quiet, mildly distended soft abdomen, no peritonism, patent anus, no syndromic features
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 4.4 mmol/L; NGT drains 25 mL bilious fluid

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange urgent upper gastrointestinal contrast study to assess duodenojejunal flexure position and rule out volvulus
options.rationale: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with midgut volvulus until proved otherwise. The diagnostic gold standard is an urgent upper GI contrast study showing the position of the duodenojejunal flexure (DJ flexure). A normal DJ flexure crosses to the left of the spine at the L2 level; an abnormal DJ flexure on the right of the spine is diagnostic of malrotation. Definitive treatment is Ladd's procedure. (Source: RCH CPG: Bilious vomiting in the neonate; RACS Position on Paediatric Surgical Emergencies)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious vomiting in the neonate
RACS Position on Paediatric Surgical Emergencies

options.text: Reassure the team and observe with intravenous maintenance fluids while continuing breastfeeding on demand
options.rationale: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is a surgical emergency; observation risks bowel necrosis from undiagnosed volvulus within hours. (Source: RCH CPG: Bilious vomiting in the neonate)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious vomiting in the neonate

options.text: Arrange supine and erect abdominal X-rays only and review again at 6 hours from the time of presentation
options.rationale: Plain abdominal X-rays may be normal early in malrotation; relying on them alone delays diagnosis. The AU pathway is urgent contrast study. (Source: RCH CPG: Bilious vomiting in the neonate)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious vomiting in the neonate

options.text: Arrange a barium enema as the first imaging modality to assess caecal position and possible obstruction
options.rationale: Barium enema can localise the caecum but does not show DJ flexure position; upper GI contrast study is the AU first-line for suspected malrotation. (Source: RACS Position on Paediatric Surgical Emergencies)
source_refs: RACS Position on Paediatric Surgical Emergencies

options.text: Stop oral feeding and start trial of probiotic for a presumed neonatal feeding intolerance with green vomits
options.rationale: Green vomits are bilious and not benign reflux or feeding intolerance; probiotic has no role in suspected malrotation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bilious vomiting in the neonate)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious vomiting in the neonate

explanation.summary: Bilious (green) vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with midgut volvulus until proved otherwise. Other differentials include duodenal atresia/stenosis, jejunal atresia, meconium ileus, Hirschsprung's disease, and necrotising enterocolitis. The diagnostic test is the urgent upper GI contrast study showing the duodenojejunal flexure position; a flexure to the right of the spine is diagnostic of malrotation. Plain X-ray showing the double bubble (with no distal gas) suggests duodenal atresia. Definitive treatment for malrotation with volvulus is Ladd's procedure: detorsion, division of Ladd's bands, broadening of the mesentery, appendicectomy, and bowel placement with small bowel right and colon left.
key_points: Bilious vomiting in neonate = malrotation with volvulus until proved otherwise
Upper GI contrast study is the diagnostic test
Normal DJ flexure to left of spine at L2 level
Definitive treatment: Ladd's procedure
explanation.pearls: Time to surgery is critical: midgut volvulus that obstructs the superior mesenteric artery can produce total intestinal necrosis within hours, leading to short-bowel syndrome or death.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Bilious vomiting in the neonate
RACS Position on Paediatric Surgical Emergencies
